name: PGI / IGP
meaning: Protected Geographical Indication. EU classification with looser rules than PDO/AOC. In France, PGI = IGP (Indication Géographique Protégée), e.g. Pays d'Oc IGP.

name: Bordeaux AOC
meaning: Region-wide Bordeaux appellation. Entry-level — a wine labelled 'Bordeaux AOC' may come from anywhere in the Bordeaux region.

name: Bordeaux Supérieur
meaning: Step up from Bordeaux AOC. Slightly stricter rules (lower yields, higher minimum alcohol). Usually red.

name: Cru Bourgeois
meaning: Classification for selected châteaux in the Médoc/Haut-Médoc, below Grand Cru Classé. Indicates a recognised level of quality.

name: Grand Cru Classé
meaning: Top classification of Bordeaux châteaux (1855 Classification of the Médoc + Sauternes; separate classifications for Saint-Émilion and Graves). Used on the label of the classified château.

name: Premier Cru / 1er Cru
meaning: Burgundy classification, second-highest after Grand Cru. Designates specific named vineyard sites (climats) within a village. Labels show the village name plus 'Premier Cru' (or '1er Cru'), often followed by the climat name.

name: Grand Cru
meaning: Highest classification. In Burgundy, designates specific named vineyard sites which appear as their own AOC (e.g. Le Chambertin). In Alsace, designates 51 specific named vineyard sites. (Note: Bordeaux uses the separate term 'Grand Cru Classé'.)

name: Joven
meaning: Spanish for 'young'. Wines with no minimum ageing requirement; usually unoaked, made for early drinking.

name: Crianza
meaning: Spanish ageing classification. For red Rioja: minimum 24 months total ageing including at least 12 months in oak.

name: Reserva
meaning: Spanish ageing classification, above Crianza. For red Rioja: minimum 36 months total ageing including at least 12 months in oak.

name: Gran Reserva
meaning: Top Spanish ageing classification. For red Rioja: minimum 60 months total ageing including at least 24 months in oak and 36 in bottle.

name: Qualitätswein
meaning: Mid-tier German PDO classification, below Prädikatswein. Wines must come from one of the 13 specified regions (Anbaugebiete) such as Mosel, Rheingau or Pfalz.

name: Prädikatswein
meaning: Top tier of German PDO wines. Subdivided into six Prädikat categories defined by grape ripeness at harvest: Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, Trockenbeerenauslese, Eiswein.

name: Kabinett
meaning: Lightest of the six German Prädikat categories. From regularly ripe grapes; can range from dry to off-dry. Often the lightest-bodied, lowest-alcohol style in a producer's range.

name: Spätlese
meaning: Late-harvest Prädikat. Riper grapes than Kabinett; can be dry to sweet.

name: Auslese
meaning: Selected late-harvest Prädikat. From very ripe selected bunches, often partly affected by botrytis; can be dry to sweet but typically sweeter than Spätlese.

name: Beerenauslese (BA)
meaning: Sweet Prädikat made from individually selected botrytis-affected berries. Always sweet, concentrated.

name: Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA)
meaning: Top sweet Prädikat. Made from dried, shrivelled, botrytis-affected berries. Very sweet and intensely concentrated.

name: Eiswein
meaning: Sweet Prädikat made from grapes frozen on the vine and pressed while still frozen. Concentrates sugars by removing water as ice. (Healthy, non-botrytised grapes.)

name: Trocken
meaning: German for 'dry'. Indicates a dry wine. Can appear on Qualitätswein and Prädikatswein labels.

name: Halbtrocken
meaning: German for 'half-dry' (off-dry). Indicates a slightly sweeter wine than Trocken.

name: Classico
meaning: Italian term for the historic, original ('classic') heart of a wine zone — typically the hilly central part. Usually higher-quality than the broader zone. Used in Chianti Classico, Soave Classico, Valpolicella Classico.

name: Riserva
meaning: Italian term indicating extended ageing requirements set by each DOC/DOCG. For Chianti Classico Riserva, minimum 24 months ageing. Generally indicates higher quality than the basic version.

name: Amarone
meaning: Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG. Made from Corvina (and other local varieties) using the appassimento method — grapes are dried for several months before fermentation, concentrating sugars and flavours. Full-bodied, dry red with high alcohol; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Cape Blend
meaning: South African label term for a red blend that contains a significant amount of Pinotage. Cape Blends typically combine Pinotage with international varieties such as Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot or Shiraz.

name: Aszú
meaning: Hungarian for botrytis-affected grapes. Tokaji Aszú is a sweet wine made from Furmint (and other local varieties) affected by botrytis/noble rot. Concentrated, sweet, with characteristic dried fruit, caramel and honey notes; outstanding ageing potential.

name: Recioto
meaning: Italian sweet wine made from grapes dried by the appassimento method. Fermentation is stopped before all the sugar is converted, leaving residual sweetness. Examples: Recioto della Valpolicella DOCG (sweet red from Corvina), Recioto di Soave (sweet white from Garganega).

name: Brut
meaning: Sweetness level for sparkling wine — dry. Up to 12 g/L residual sugar. The most common sweetness for Champagne, Cava and Prosecco.

name: Demi-Sec
meaning: Sweetness level for sparkling wine — medium-sweet. 32–50 g/L residual sugar. Sweeter than Brut and Extra Dry.

name: Traditional Method
meaning: Method for making sparkling wine where the second fermentation takes place in the bottle in which the wine is sold. Used for Champagne, Cava, and Crémant. Develops fine mousse and biscuity/autolytic character with extended lees ageing.

name: Vintage
meaning: Sparkling wine made from grapes of a single year, declared on the label. In Champagne, vintage wines are made only in the best years and have longer minimum lees ageing than non-vintage. Generally higher quality and price.

name: Non-Vintage (NV)
meaning: Sparkling wine blended from base wines of multiple years to maintain a consistent house style. The majority of Champagne is non-vintage.

name: Méthode Cap Classique (MCC)
meaning: South African term for Traditional Method sparkling wine (second fermentation in bottle). Used because 'Champagne' and 'Méthode Champenoise' are protected.

name: Fino
meaning: Driest style of Sherry. Made from Palomino, fortified to ~15% abv (low enough for flor to develop). Aged biologically under flor — a film of yeast that protects the wine from oxidation and gives bread/dough/saline notes. Pale, dry, light-bodied. Manzanilla is a Fino made specifically in Sanlúcar de Barrameda.

name: Amontillado
meaning: Style of Sherry that begins life as Fino (aged under flor) but is then refortified to ~17% abv, killing the flor and continuing to age oxidatively. Combines biological and oxidative ageing — amber, nutty, dry.

name: Oloroso
meaning: Style of Sherry fortified to ~17% abv from the start, so flor never develops. Aged oxidatively only — deep brown, full-bodied, with dried-fruit and walnut notes. Naturally dry; sweetened versions are sold as Cream Sherry.

name: Pale Cream
meaning: Sweetened Fino — Fino Sherry with sweetening agents (rectified concentrated grape must) added. Pale colour, fresh, lightly sweet.

name: Medium
meaning: Sweetened Amontillado — Amontillado Sherry blended with sweet wine. Amber colour, medium-sweet, nutty.

name: Cream
meaning: Sweetened Oloroso — Oloroso Sherry blended with sweet wine (often Pedro Ximénez). Deep brown, sweet, full-bodied.

name: Pedro Ximénez (PX)
meaning: Both a grape variety and the name of a very sweet Sherry made from sun-dried Pedro Ximénez grapes. Intensely sweet, dark, with raisin, fig, treacle and coffee notes.

name: Ruby
meaning: Entry-level style of Port. Aged briefly in large vessels to retain youthful, fruity character. Deep red, fruity, simple. Made for early drinking; no benefit from further bottle age.

name: Reserve Ruby
meaning: Premium Ruby Port — a blend of better-quality young wines. Fuller and richer than basic Ruby; still made for early drinking.

name: Late Bottled Vintage (LBV)
meaning: Port from a single (undeclared) vintage, aged 4–6 years in vessel before bottling. Fruitier and more accessible than Vintage Port; ready to drink on release.

name: Vintage Port
meaning: Top tier of Port. Made only in declared vintages (the best years, ~3 in a decade). Aged just 2–3 years in vessel then bottled. Develops in bottle for decades, throwing a heavy sediment. Concentrated, full-bodied, with outstanding ageing potential.

name: Tawny
meaning: Oxidatively aged Port. Long ageing in small wooden vessels turns the wine tawny-brown and develops dried-fruit, nut, caramel and toffee notes. Sold with age indications: 10, 20, 30 or 40 Year Old (the average age of the blend). Ready to drink on release.
